model_summary: Targeting nocturnal perseverative cognition and its sleep/circadian consequences, this initial observation model uses 5 complaint-derived criteria (nighttime replay of past conversations, anticipatory worry/list-making, global nighttime cognitive hyperarousal, delayed sleep onset, and morning interpersonal irritability) and 8 modifiable predictors drawn from high-signal circadian and CBTi-style levers (morning bright light exposure, evening screen minutes, evening wind-down adherence, expressive journaling, stimulus-control adherence, scheduled worry-time use, sleep-environment quality, and morning stabilization routine). Measurements combine a single morning diary (captures full-night phenomena and morning state), two evening momentary EMAs (capture evening behaviors/strategies), and passive nightly screen-time aggregation. Design emphasizes 1–9 Likert where appropriate, continuous minutes for behaviors, low redundancy across predictors, and a 3-week collection window to support within-person gVAR while preserving feasibility.
variable_selection_notes: Criteria: all five complaint-derived constructs were retained to preserve complaint resolution and downstream causal paths (replay vs worry vs hyperarousal → sleep onset → morning irritability). Predictors: selected top, actionable nodes prioritized by HyDe and LLM mapping focusing on circadian entrainment (morning light), evening stimulus reduction (screen time, wind-down), cognitive unloading (journaling, scheduled worry), bed/stimulus control, sleep environment, and morning-anchor routines. Trade-offs: limited to 8 predictors to keep gVAR estimable and reduce collinearity; excluded higher-cost/medical nodes (TMS, pharmacotherapy, implantables) at this stage. Bias/uncertainty notes: relevance estimates rely on general CBTi and circadian literature plus ranking signals; individual response heterogeneity and measurement error (self-report vs passive) are expected.

momentary_ema.notes: Two momentary prompts capture evening presleep behaviors/strategies and a daytime check for confounders. Evening prompt targets wind-down routine, journaling, worry-time, and bed-use adherence.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures 'last night' cognitive activity (replay, worry, hyperarousal), sleep onset time, sleep environment rated retrospectively, and current morning irritability.

passive_streams.notes: Passive aggregation of device screen-time minutes in the 2 hours before reported bedtime each night; aggregated per calendar day / night for linkage with morning diary.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Three-week, mixed EMA+passive design yields ~63 self-report observations (42 momentary + 21 diaries) plus 21 passive daily aggregates. This gives enough temporal resolution for gVAR with 8 predictors and 5 criteria when using within-person standardization and modest regularization.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 Likert for subjective constructs to increase within-person variance. Continuous minutes for screen time and journaling to capture graded effects. Expectable day-to-day variability in nocturnal measures and predictors (esp. screen time, routines).
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Morning diary critical—higher missingness risk on weekends or nights with late sleep. Mitigate with reminder scheduling, make morning diary window broad (e.g., until 10:30) and allow retrospective reporting for previous night. Passive screen-time is robust to missingness but may be unavailable for some OS levels; plan per-subject fallback (self-report evening screen minutes).

safety_notes: No acute self-harm or psychosis signals are present in the complaint text. If assessments reveal severe worsening (e.g., very high distress, suicidal ideation, or functional impairment), follow local clinical escalation procedures. Encourage participants to contact a health professional if sleep loss or daily functioning becomes severe; provide crisis resources as per platform safety policy.

criteria_variables.label: Nighttime replay of past conversations

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how much did you replay past conversations in your head as if they were happening now?
measurement.notes: Single morning diary item covering the full night's replay episodes to maximize capture of events that occur after evening prompts.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider winsorize at 1–99 percentile if extreme skew

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / sleep-onset insomnia / pre_sleep_rumination_frequency
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / sleep-onset insomnia / *pre_sleep_rumination_frequency
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable describes nocturnal cognitive rumination about past conversations (replaying conversations in one’s head). This best maps to the leaf pre_sleep_rumination_frequency (insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / sleep-onset insomnia / pre_sleep_rumination_frequency), which captures frequency of rumination around sleep onset. The measurement item (morning diary capturing last night) aligns with capturing nocturnal cognitive arousal. Among candidates, this is the most specific operational construct matching nocturnal rumination preceding or occurring around sleep onset, rather than post-awakening or explicit sleep behaviors like somniloquy. Therefore it is the closest leaf match while remaining within the pseudoprofile’s CBTi/circadian framework.
criteria_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Nighttime replay of past conversations | Operational item/signal: Last night, how much did you replay past conversations in your head as if they were happening now? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Single morning diary item covering the full night's replay episodes to maximize capture of events that occur after evening prompts.

criteria_variables.label: Nighttime anticipatory worry / list-making

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how often did you generate lists or imagine negative outcomes for the near future (e.g., tomorrow)?
measurement.notes: Captures future-oriented perseverative cognition distinct from past-oriented replay.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / anxiety disorders / generalized anxiety disorder / with cognitive rumination / catastrophizing_rumination_content
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / anxiety disorders / generalized anxiety disorder / with cognitive rumination / *catastrophizing_rumination_content
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable maps to nocturnal anticipatory cognitive activity (future-oriented worry with cognitive rumination). The most specific matching leaf is 'catastrophizing_rumination_content' under CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / anxiety disorders / generalized anxiety disorder / with cognitive rumination / catastrophizing_rumination_content, which directly captures listing/imagining negative future outcomes. This aligns with the nocturnal perseverative cognition target and with the measurement item (last night) and EMA/self-report diary timing. It is more specific than general 'worry' leaves and matches the content focus of the pseudoprofile's criteria (nighttime worry/unloading strategies).

criteria_variables.label: Nighttime cognitive hyperarousal

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how mentally activated or unable to quiet your thoughts did you feel overall?
measurement.notes: Global hyperarousal rating to capture nonspecific activation that sustains replay/worry.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider AR effects (autocorrelation) modeling

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / sleep-maintenance insomnia / nocturnal_cognitive_arousal
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / sleep-maintenance insomnia / *nocturnal_cognitive_arousal
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf is nocturnal_cognitive_arousal (nocturnal cognitive arousal) because var C03 asks about nighttime mental activation and inability to quiet thoughts last night, on a 1–9 scale. This directly maps to nocturnal cognitive arousal as a nocturnal sleep-wake symptom and aligns with the measurement being EMA self-report during the full-night window captured by the morning diary. It is the most specific operational construct among candidates for nighttime cognitive hyperarousal.

criteria_variables.label: Delayed sleep onset (late sleep timing)

measurement.channel: morning_diary (smartphone EMA app); optional passive sleep-detection
measurement.item_or_signal: What time did you fall asleep last night? (enter HH:MM). If unknown, estimate.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: clock time (HH:MM) — will be transformed to minutes-since-midnight and to circular measures
measurement.notes: Prefer self-report sleep onset; augment with passive sleep-onset detection where available to validate.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: convert clock-time to continuous minutes; use circular transform (sine/cosine) or within-person z; evaluate and model daily drift

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders / delayed sleep–wake phase disorder / delayed_sleep_onset_time
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders / delayed sleep–wake phase disorder / *delayed_sleep_onset_time
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable C04 asks for the clock time of sleep onset last night (via EMA self-report). This maps to the leaf 'delayed_sleep_onset_time' under circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders / delayed sleep–wake phase disorder, which operationalizes late sleep timing as a specific onset time (HH:MM). The measurement converts HH:MM to minutes-since-midnight and circular measures, aligning with the leaf's endpoint. The pseudoprofile emphasizes circadian entrainment and delayed sleep onset, supporting this mapping without overfitting to other latency or misperception constructs.

criteria_variables.label: Morning interpersonal irritability

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, how irritated or hostile toward others do you feel?
measurement.notes: Momentary morning affect measure anchored to ~08:00 to capture complaint-specific timing.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / disruptive, impulse-control, and conduct disorders / disruptive behavior disorders / oppositional defiant disorder / presentation pattern / predominantly angry or irritable / irritable_mood_present
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / disruptive, impulse-control, and conduct disorders / disruptive behavior disorders / oppositional defiant disorder / presentation pattern / predominantly angry or irritable / *irritable_mood_present
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-match leaf: irritable_mood_present (idx 24228). The variable Morning interpersonal irritability is a momentary self-reported irritability state directed toward others in the morning. The leaf irritable_mood_present represents an irritable mood state (often angry/irritable) and aligns with a momentary, self-report measurement of irritability. The measurement mode EMA_self_report with a 1–9 scale fits the data collection design (morning diary/EMA at ~08:00). While not explicitly labeled interpersonal, irritability mood is the closest operational construct to the targeted morning interpersonal irritability toward others. Other leaves are either broader mood constructs or refer to different time frames or clinical contexts.
criteria_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Morning interpersonal irritability | Operational item/signal: Right now, how irritated or hostile toward others do you feel? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Momentary morning affect measure anchored to ~08:00 to capture complaint-specific timing.

predictor_variables.label: Morning bright light exposure (minutes within 1h of waking)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Morning bright light is a primary circadian cue that advances circadian phase, supports earlier sleep onset over days, and can improve morning mood/alertness.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Spend 10–30 minutes outdoors or next to a bright window within 60 minutes of waking.

measurement.item_or_signal: How many minutes did you spend outdoors or in bright indoor light within the first hour after waking?
measurement.notes: If passive ambient/light sensor available, supplement but self-report is primary.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; clip extreme values (e.g., >180 min)

expected_effects.rationale: Phase-advancing light reduces delayed sleep over days, indirectly reducing replay by improving night timing; effect on past-replay is indirect and slower.
Entrainment reduces evening vulnerability to future-oriented worry through stabilized sleep-wake timing over days.
Better circadian alignment reduces global nighttime hyperarousal across nights.
Consistent morning light exposure tends to advance circadian phase and reduce delayed sleep onset over days to weeks.
Acute morning light and activity can improve immediate morning mood and reduce irritability.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility via morning diary; passive augmentation possible.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Direct match: Morning bright light exposure leaf at BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / circadian_entrainment / light_cues / morning_bright_light_exposure. The variable label and measurement item specify minutes spent in bright light within the first hour after waking, which corresponds to the measurement item: 'How many minutes did you spend outdoors or in bright indoor light within the first hour after waking?' The mechanism: morning light is a primary circadian cue advancing circadian phase and improving morning alertness. The measurement mode EMA_self_report via morning_diary aligns with the leaf's measurement path. This leaf is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this predictor.

predictor_variables.label: Evening screen minutes (minutes of screen-on in 2h before sleep)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Evening screen exposure (light and stimulating content) can increase arousal and delay melatonin onset and sleep onset; screen time minutes provide objective passive measure of this exposure.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Limit screen use or enable full-night blue-light filters in the 2 hours before intended bedtime.

measurement.channel: phone OS screen-time API or smartphone passive logging (minutes of screen-on between bedtime-2h and bedtime)
measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes of screen-on time in the 2 hours before reported bedtime (device-reported).
measurement.recall_window: night prior (2-hour window ending at reported bedtime)
measurement.notes: If OS-level screen time unavailable, fallback to evening EMA self-report minutes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p for skew; within-person z-score for gVAR

expected_effects.rationale: More evening screen exposure (minutes) tends to be associated with greater nocturnal intrusive replay via heightened arousal and engagement with emotional content.
Evening screens can amplify future-oriented worry through engagement, leading to more list-making at night.
Screen use increases cognitive activation, producing higher nighttime hyperarousal the same night.
Higher evening screen minutes are associated with later sleep onset that night (or delayed phase over time).
More evening screen time may contribute to worse sleep and therefore to increased morning irritability.

feasibility.notes: Passive but may be missing for some operating systems; self-report fallback recommended.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf: late_evening_screen_reduction (6325). The variable label and measurement item (Minutes of screen-on time in the 2 hours before bedtime) align with late-evening_screen_reduction, a predictor capturing reduced late-evening screen exposure to support circadian entrainment via light cues. Mechanism: screen use increases arousal and delays melatonin/sleep onset; this leaf represents the targeted operational construct to measure and reduce that exposure. It is more specific than alternatives like screen_night_mode or presleep_downshift.

predictor_variables.label: Evening wind-down routine adherence (1–9)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: A consistent low-stimulation wind-down reduces cognitive activation before bed and supports conditioned sleep onset.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 30–60 minute low-stimulation routine (warm shower, reading non-stimulating material, dim lights).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you follow your planned wind-down routine?
measurement.notes: Evening adherence item intended to vary nightly; avoids overlap with morning diary.

expected_effects.rationale: Better wind-down adherence should reduce immediate night replay intensity by lowering cognitive stimulation before bed.
Helps contain future-oriented worry acutely that night.
Lower arousal via downshift reduces global hyperarousal that night.
Promotes earlier sleep onset the same night through behavioral conditioning and reduced stimulation.
Improved wind-down can produce better sleep and reduced next-morning irritability.

feasibility.notes: High compliance expected when item short and anchoring clear.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label matches the leaf wind_down_routine under presleep_downshift. It is the most specific operational construct for an evening wind-down adherence measure. Mechanism aligns with the node: a consistent low-stimulation wind-down reduces cognitive activation before bed to support sleep onset. Measurements align: EMA_self_report, evening_prompt, since_last_prompt, 1–9 Likert scale, and item queries adherence to planned wind-down routine. This fits the pseudoprofile context’s emphasis on circadian entrainment and CBTi-style presleep routines, without overfitting to non-operational aspects.

predictor_variables.label: Evening cognitive unloading / expressive journaling (minutes)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Brief expressive writing or 'brain-dump' can offload ruminative content and reduce rehearsal of conversations/worries at night.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 10–20 minutes of structured worry-journaling or 'brain dump' before wind-down.

measurement.item_or_signal: Did you do a cognitive unloading exercise (journaling/brain-dump) tonight? If yes, how many minutes did you spend?
measurement.notes: Use minutes rather than binary to increase variance; follow-up helpfulness item optional.

expected_effects.rationale: Direct offloading expected to reduce replay content the same night.
Reduces list-making/worry by containing anticipatory thoughts.
May reduce global activation through cognitive unloading.
By lowering pre-sleep cognitive load, may facilitate earlier sleep onset that night.
Improved sleep quality from unloading can reduce morning irritability.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: If journaling surfaces intense distress, include guidance to pause and use grounding techniques; escalate via safety protocol if necessary.

feasibility.notes: Self-reported minutes easily collected; may be zero-inflated some nights.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is cognitive_unloading_journaling (PSYCHO) which directly corresponds to 'Evening cognitive unloading / expressive journaling' and uses the measurement item 'Did you do a cognitive unloading exercise (journaling/brain-dump) tonight? predictor_variables.label: Bed-use / stimulus-control adherence (1–9)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Using bed only for sleep/sex (avoiding work, phone, eating) strengthens bed–sleep association and reduces conditioned arousal in bed.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Avoid phone/TV/work in bed; only go to bed when sleepy; leave bed if unable to sleep.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how well did you avoid non-sleep activities in bed (phone, work, eating)?
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1–9 Likert (1 = not at all, 9 = always avoided)
measurement.notes: Targets conditioned arousal directly; may be lower variability if habit strong.

expected_effects.rationale: Reducing bed-based stimulation can decrease conditioned replay in bed (moderate effect).
May modestly reduce anticipatory worry in bed by removing triggers.
Reduced engagement in bed should lower in-bed arousal.
Stimulus control tends to improve sleep onset by strengthening bed-sleep association over days.
Indirect effect via improved sleep quality; likely small-to-moderate.

feasibility.notes: Behavioral adherence items may show ceiling/floor effects in some individuals.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable Bed-use / stimulus-control adherence maps to the leaf stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning, which aligns with the intended operant conditioning mechanism: reducing non-sleep activities in bed to strengthen bed-sleep association and reduce conditioned arousal. The ontology_path shows sleep-related cognitive-behavioral interventions -> conditioning_and_associations -> relearning -> stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning, matching the variable’s focus. Measurement context (EMA self-report in the evening assessing adherence since the last prompt) supports this leaf as the most specific predictor construct for bed-based stimulus control. The pseudoprofile notes corroborate focus on CBTi-style levers like stimulus-control adherence, making this the best match among candidates.

predictor_variables.label: Use of scheduled worry time (1–9)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: A scheduled daytime worry period ('worry time') can contain and relocate future-oriented worry away from bedtime.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 10–20 minute scheduled worry slot earlier in the day to capture and plan responses to worries.

measurement.item_or_signal: Did you use a scheduled 'worry time' today? (1 = not at all, 9 = fully used as planned)
measurement.notes: Designed to capture containment strategy use earlier in the day that may protect evening.

expected_effects.rationale: Containing worries earlier reduces the likelihood of nightly replay; effect moderate.
Directly targets future-oriented worry with large expected effect on same evening.
Reducing worry content reduces cognitive activation at night.
May modestly facilitate sleep onset the same night by lowering pre-sleep worry.
Indirectly reduces morning irritability via improved sleep quality.

feasibility.notes: May be subject to demand characteristics; short item reduces burden.

The ontology_path explicitly ends with before_bed_worksheet, aligning with the scheduled worry concept, and the measurement item (Did you use a scheduled 'worry time' today?; 1–9 Likert) aligns with applying this construct in an EMA evening prompt. This makes it the most specific, operational leaf for this variable among the candidates.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep environment quality (darkness, temperature, noise) 1–9
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Physical sleep environment influences sleep initiation and maintenance and thereby nighttime arousal and morning mood.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Blackout curtains, earplugs, cooler bedroom, reduce ambient light/noise.

measurement.item_or_signal: How optimal was your sleep environment last night (darkness, temperature, noise)?
measurement.notes: Single composite item to limit burden and capture environment-related variance.

expected_effects.rationale: A better environment reduces disturbances that can trigger replay; effect modest.
A better environment reduces opportunities for worry-associated awakenings.
Improved environment reduces physiological/sensory arousal during night.
Better environment supports earlier sleep onset and sleep continuity.
Better environment tends to improve sleep and reduce next-morning irritability.

feasibility.notes: Single composite is practical and reduces participant burden.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-match leaf is within BIO sleep_environment domain, specifically light_and_display_control with *low_lux_bedroom_lighting. The variable Sleep environment quality (darkness, temperature, noise) 1–9 is a composite of darkness, etc. Lighting darkness is the most directly captured predictor among candidates; although it doesn't cover temperature/noise, it represents the most specific, actionable operational construct aligned to the darkness aspect of the sleep environment and aligns with the morning-diary self-report measurement of last night’s environment. The measurement mode (EMA_self_report in morning diary) fits the intention of a self-reported environment quality item; thus this is the best leaf under the constraints.

predictor_variables.label: Morning stabilization routine (light + gentle activity) 1–9
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: A consistent morning anchor (light + gentle mobility) stabilizes circadian rhythms and improves morning mood/regulation.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Within 60 min of waking: 10–20 min outside or bright light + gentle stretches/walk.

measurement.item_or_signal: Within the first hour after waking, did you do a morning stabilization routine (light + gentle activity)?
measurement.notes: Expected to have relatively high immediate influence on morning irritability.

expected_effects.rationale: By stabilizing circadian timing, may indirectly reduce night replay over days.
Indirect effect via improved daytime regulation and sleep timing.
Circadian entrainment supports lower evening hyperarousal over time.
Morning anchor supports phase advance over days to weeks, improving sleep onset timing over time.
Strong immediate effect expected on morning irritability (within hours).

feasibility.notes: Morning items tend to have some missingness but are key to morning affect modeling.

The measurement item asks about performing morning stabilization within an hour of waking, matching this leaf's scope. The mechanism aligns with stabilizing circadian timing and morning mood regulation through a consistent light-based anchor.

expected_collinearity_risks: P03 (wind-down), P04 (journaling), and P06 (scheduled worry) measure overlapping presleep cognitive-behavioral strategies and may be collinear; consider principal components or penalized gVAR if needed.
P01 and P08 both index circadian anchors (morning light vs routine) and could correlate; treat as distinct but monitor collinearity.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: Stimulus-control (P05) and environment (P07) items may show ceiling effects if participant already highly adherent; journaling minutes (P04) may be zero-inflated on many nights.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert anchors to increase resolution on subjective items.
Collect continuous minutes for P01, P02, P04 to maximize within-person variance.
Allow optional short free-text on evenings with extreme scores to aid qualitative interpretation (not required for gVAR).
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Neuromodulation (TMS, tES), pharmacotherapy (sleep meds, psychotropics), implantable devices — excluded from initial gVAR due to feasibility, cost, and safety constraints.
Extensive biometric streams (HRV continuous) deprioritized initially to reduce complexity.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 5 criteria and 8 predictors measured at daily resolution (3 self-report obs/day + passive daily aggregates), the dataset across 21 days should allow within-person gVAR estimation when applying within-person standardization, regularization (LASSO/graphical LASSO), and limiting maximal lag (e.g., 0–24h / 1-day lag). Monitor missingness and reduce multicollinearity among evening strategy items if needed.

criteria.label: Nighttime replay of past conversations
criteria.query_text_used: Nighttime replay of past conversations | Operational item/signal: Last night, how much did you replay past conversations in your head as if they were happening now? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Single morning diary item covering the full night's replay episodes to maximize capture of events that occur after evening prompts.

mapping.rationale: The variable describes nocturnal cognitive rumination about past conversations (replaying conversations in one’s head). This best maps to the leaf pre_sleep_rumination_frequency (insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / sleep-onset insomnia / pre_sleep_rumination_frequency), which captures frequency of rumination around sleep onset. The measurement item (morning diary capturing last night) aligns with capturing nocturnal cognitive arousal. Among candidates, this is the most specific operational construct matching nocturnal rumination preceding or occurring around sleep onset, rather than post-awakening or explicit sleep behaviors like somniloquy. Therefore it is the closest leaf match while remaining within the pseudoprofile’s CBTi/circadian framework.

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how much did you replay past conversations in your head as if they were happening now?
measurement.notes: Single morning diary item covering the full night's replay episodes to maximize capture of events that occur after evening prompts.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider winsorize at 1–99 percentile if extreme skew

criteria.label: Nighttime anticipatory worry / list-making
criteria.query_text_used: Nighttime anticipatory worry / list-making | Operational item/signal: Last night, how often did you generate lists or imagine negative outcomes for the near future (e.g., tomorrow)? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures future-oriented perseverative cognition distinct from past-oriented replay.

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to nocturnal anticipatory cognitive activity (future-oriented worry with cognitive rumination). The most specific matching leaf is 'catastrophizing_rumination_content' under CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / anxiety disorders / generalized anxiety disorder / with cognitive rumination / catastrophizing_rumination_content, which directly captures listing/imagining negative future outcomes. This aligns with the nocturnal perseverative cognition target and with the measurement item (last night) and EMA/self-report diary timing. It is more specific than general 'worry' leaves and matches the content focus of the pseudoprofile's criteria (nighttime worry/unloading strategies).

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how often did you generate lists or imagine negative outcomes for the near future (e.g., tomorrow)?
measurement.notes: Captures future-oriented perseverative cognition distinct from past-oriented replay.

criteria.label: Nighttime cognitive hyperarousal
criteria.query_text_used: Nighttime cognitive hyperarousal | Operational item/signal: Last night, how mentally activated or unable to quiet your thoughts did you feel overall? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Global hyperarousal rating to capture nonspecific activation that sustains replay/worry.

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is nocturnal_cognitive_arousal (nocturnal cognitive arousal) because var C03 asks about nighttime mental activation and inability to quiet thoughts last night, on a 1–9 scale. This directly maps to nocturnal cognitive arousal as a nocturnal sleep-wake symptom and aligns with the measurement being EMA self-report during the full-night window captured by the morning diary. It is the most specific operational construct among candidates for nighttime cognitive hyperarousal.

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how mentally activated or unable to quiet your thoughts did you feel overall?
measurement.notes: Global hyperarousal rating to capture nonspecific activation that sustains replay/worry.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider AR effects (autocorrelation) modeling

criteria.label: Delayed sleep onset (late sleep timing)
criteria.query_text_used: Delayed sleep onset (late sleep timing) | Operational item/signal: What time did you fall asleep last night? (enter HH:MM). If unknown, estimate. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Prefer self-report sleep onset; augment with passive sleep-onset detection where available to validate.

mapping.rationale: Variable C04 asks for the clock time of sleep onset last night (via EMA self-report). This maps to the leaf 'delayed_sleep_onset_time' under circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders / delayed sleep–wake phase disorder, which operationalizes late sleep timing as a specific onset time (HH:MM). The measurement converts HH:MM to minutes-since-midnight and circular measures, aligning with the leaf's endpoint. The pseudoprofile emphasizes circadian entrainment and delayed sleep onset, supporting this mapping without overfitting to other latency or misperception constructs.

measurement.channel: morning_diary (smartphone EMA app); optional passive sleep-detection
measurement.item_or_signal: What time did you fall asleep last night? (enter HH:MM). If unknown, estimate.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: clock time (HH:MM) — will be transformed to minutes-since-midnight and to circular measures
measurement.notes: Prefer self-report sleep onset; augment with passive sleep-onset detection where available to validate.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: convert clock-time to continuous minutes; use circular transform (sine/cosine) or within-person z; evaluate and model daily drift

criteria.label: Morning interpersonal irritability
criteria.query_text_used: Morning interpersonal irritability | Operational item/signal: Right now, how irritated or hostile toward others do you feel? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Momentary morning affect measure anchored to ~08:00 to capture complaint-specific timing.

mapping.rationale: Best-match leaf: irritable_mood_present (idx 24228). The variable Morning interpersonal irritability is a momentary self-reported irritability state directed toward others in the morning. The leaf irritable_mood_present represents an irritable mood state (often angry/irritable) and aligns with a momentary, self-report measurement of irritability. The measurement mode EMA_self_report with a 1–9 scale fits the data collection design (morning diary/EMA at ~08:00). While not explicitly labeled interpersonal, irritability mood is the closest operational construct to the targeted morning interpersonal irritability toward others. Other leaves are either broader mood constructs or refer to different time frames or clinical contexts.

predictors.label: Morning bright light exposure (minutes within 1h of waking)
predictors.query_text_used: Morning bright light exposure (minutes within 1h of waking) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Morning bright light is a primary circadian cue that advances circadian phase, supports earlier sleep onset over days, and can improve morning mood/alertness. | Intervention examples: Spend 10–30 minutes outdoors or next to a bright window within 60 minutes of waking. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How many minutes did you spend outdoors or in bright indoor light within the first hour after waking? | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / circadian_entrainment / light_cues / *morning_bright_light_exposure

mapping.rationale: Direct match: Morning bright light exposure leaf at BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / circadian_entrainment / light_cues / morning_bright_light_exposure. The variable label and measurement item specify minutes spent in bright light within the first hour after waking, which corresponds to the measurement item: 'How many minutes did you spend outdoors or in bright indoor light within the first hour after waking?' The mechanism: morning light is a primary circadian cue advancing circadian phase and improving morning alertness. The measurement mode EMA_self_report via morning_diary aligns with the leaf's measurement path. This leaf is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this predictor.

source_variable.label: Morning bright light exposure (minutes within 1h of waking)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Morning bright light is a primary circadian cue that advances circadian phase, supports earlier sleep onset over days, and can improve morning mood/alertness.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Spend 10–30 minutes outdoors or next to a bright window within 60 minutes of waking.

measurement.item_or_signal: How many minutes did you spend outdoors or in bright indoor light within the first hour after waking?
measurement.notes: If passive ambient/light sensor available, supplement but self-report is primary.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; clip extreme values (e.g., >180 min)

expected_effects.rationale: Phase-advancing light reduces delayed sleep over days, indirectly reducing replay by improving night timing; effect on past-replay is indirect and slower.
Entrainment reduces evening vulnerability to future-oriented worry through stabilized sleep-wake timing over days.
Better circadian alignment reduces global nighttime hyperarousal across nights.
Consistent morning light exposure tends to advance circadian phase and reduce delayed sleep onset over days to weeks.
Acute morning light and activity can improve immediate morning mood and reduce irritability.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility via morning diary; passive augmentation possible.

predictors.label: Evening screen minutes (minutes of screen-on in 2h before sleep)
predictors.query_text_used: Evening screen minutes (minutes of screen-on in 2h before sleep) | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Evening screen exposure (light and stimulating content) can increase arousal and delay melatonin onset and sleep onset; screen time minutes provide objective passive measure of this exposure. | Intervention examples: Limit screen use or enable full-night blue-light filters in the 2 hours before intended bedtime. | Measurement: platform_api | Minutes of screen-on time in the 2 hours before reported bedtime (device-reported). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / circadian_entrainment / light_cues / *late_evening_screen_reduction

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: late_evening_screen_reduction (6325). The variable label and measurement item (Minutes of screen-on time in the 2 hours before bedtime) align with late-evening_screen_reduction, a predictor capturing reduced late-evening screen exposure to support circadian entrainment via light cues. Mechanism: screen use increases arousal and delays melatonin/sleep onset; this leaf represents the targeted operational construct to measure and reduce that exposure. It is more specific than alternatives like screen_night_mode or presleep_downshift.

source_variable.label: Evening screen minutes (minutes of screen-on in 2h before sleep)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Evening screen exposure (light and stimulating content) can increase arousal and delay melatonin onset and sleep onset; screen time minutes provide objective passive measure of this exposure.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Limit screen use or enable full-night blue-light filters in the 2 hours before intended bedtime.

measurement.channel: phone OS screen-time API or smartphone passive logging (minutes of screen-on between bedtime-2h and bedtime)
measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes of screen-on time in the 2 hours before reported bedtime (device-reported).
measurement.recall_window: night prior (2-hour window ending at reported bedtime)
measurement.notes: If OS-level screen time unavailable, fallback to evening EMA self-report minutes.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p for skew; within-person z-score for gVAR

expected_effects.rationale: More evening screen exposure (minutes) tends to be associated with greater nocturnal intrusive replay via heightened arousal and engagement with emotional content.
Evening screens can amplify future-oriented worry through engagement, leading to more list-making at night.
Screen use increases cognitive activation, producing higher nighttime hyperarousal the same night.
Higher evening screen minutes are associated with later sleep onset that night (or delayed phase over time).
More evening screen time may contribute to worse sleep and therefore to increased morning irritability.

feasibility.notes: Passive but may be missing for some operating systems; self-report fallback recommended.

predictors.label: Evening wind-down routine adherence (1–9)
predictors.query_text_used: Evening wind-down routine adherence (1–9) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: A consistent low-stimulation wind-down reduces cognitive activation before bed and supports conditioned sleep onset. | Intervention examples: 30–60 minute low-stimulation routine (warm shower, reading non-stimulating material, dim lights). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, to what extent did you follow your planned wind-down routine? | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_routines_and_practices / presleep_downshift / *wind_down_routine

mapping.rationale: Variable label matches the leaf wind_down_routine under presleep_downshift. It is the most specific operational construct for an evening wind-down adherence measure. Mechanism aligns with the node: a consistent low-stimulation wind-down reduces cognitive activation before bed to support sleep onset. Measurements align: EMA_self_report, evening_prompt, since_last_prompt, 1–9 Likert scale, and item queries adherence to planned wind-down routine. This fits the pseudoprofile context’s emphasis on circadian entrainment and CBTi-style presleep routines, without overfitting to non-operational aspects.

source_variable.mechanism_rationale: A consistent low-stimulation wind-down reduces cognitive activation before bed and supports conditioned sleep onset.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: 30–60 minute low-stimulation routine (warm shower, reading non-stimulating material, dim lights).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you follow your planned wind-down routine?
measurement.notes: Evening adherence item intended to vary nightly; avoids overlap with morning diary.

expected_effects.rationale: Better wind-down adherence should reduce immediate night replay intensity by lowering cognitive stimulation before bed.
Helps contain future-oriented worry acutely that night.
Lower arousal via downshift reduces global hyperarousal that night.
Promotes earlier sleep onset the same night through behavioral conditioning and reduced stimulation.
Improved wind-down can produce better sleep and reduced next-morning irritability.

feasibility.notes: High compliance expected when item short and anchoring clear.

If yes, how many minutes did you spend? | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / presleep_downshift / *cognitive_unloading_journaling

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is cognitive_unloading_journaling (PSYCHO) which directly corresponds to 'Evening cognitive unloading / expressive journaling' and uses the measurement item 'Did you do a cognitive unloading exercise (journaling/brain-dump) tonight? If yes, how many minutes did you spend?' with minutes as the unit. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates, aligning with the evening cognitive unloading intent and the EMA_self_report modality. Mechanism rationale aligns with offloading rumination via expressive writing, reducing rehearsal of conversations/worries at night, consistent with the pseudoprofile's focus on evening cognitive unloading and CBTi circadian entrainment themes.

source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Brief expressive writing or 'brain-dump' can offload ruminative content and reduce rehearsal of conversations/worries at night.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: 10–20 minutes of structured worry-journaling or 'brain dump' before wind-down.

measurement.item_or_signal: Did you do a cognitive unloading exercise (journaling/brain-dump) tonight? If yes, how many minutes did you spend?
measurement.notes: Use minutes rather than binary to increase variance; follow-up helpfulness item optional.

expected_effects.rationale: Direct offloading expected to reduce replay content the same night.
Reduces list-making/worry by containing anticipatory thoughts.
May reduce global activation through cognitive unloading.
By lowering pre-sleep cognitive load, may facilitate earlier sleep onset that night.
Improved sleep quality from unloading can reduce morning irritability.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: If journaling surfaces intense distress, include guidance to pause and use grounding techniques; escalate via safety protocol if necessary.

feasibility.notes: Self-reported minutes easily collected; may be zero-inflated some nights.

predictors.label: Bed-use / stimulus-control adherence (1–9)
predictors.query_text_used: Bed-use / stimulus-control adherence (1–9) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Using bed only for sleep/sex (avoiding work, phone, eating) strengthens bed–sleep association and reduces conditioned arousal in bed. | Intervention examples: Avoid phone/TV/work in bed; only go to bed when sleepy; leave bed if unable to sleep. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, how well did you avoid non-sleep activities in bed (phone, work, eating)? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / conditioning_and_associations / relearning / *stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning

mapping.rationale: The variable Bed-use / stimulus-control adherence maps to the leaf stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning, which aligns with the intended operant conditioning mechanism: reducing non-sleep activities in bed to strengthen bed-sleep association and reduce conditioned arousal. The ontology_path shows sleep-related cognitive-behavioral interventions -> conditioning_and_associations -> relearning -> stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning, matching the variable’s focus. Measurement context (EMA self-report in the evening assessing adherence since the last prompt) supports this leaf as the most specific predictor construct for bed-based stimulus control. The pseudoprofile notes corroborate focus on CBTi-style levers like stimulus-control adherence, making this the best match among candidates.

source_variable.label: Bed-use / stimulus-control adherence (1–9)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Using bed only for sleep/sex (avoiding work, phone, eating) strengthens bed–sleep association and reduces conditioned arousal in bed.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Avoid phone/TV/work in bed; only go to bed when sleepy; leave bed if unable to sleep.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how well did you avoid non-sleep activities in bed (phone, work, eating)?
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1–9 Likert (1 = not at all, 9 = always avoided)
measurement.notes: Targets conditioned arousal directly; may be lower variability if habit strong.

expected_effects.rationale: Reducing bed-based stimulation can decrease conditioned replay in bed (moderate effect).
May modestly reduce anticipatory worry in bed by removing triggers.
Reduced engagement in bed should lower in-bed arousal.
Stimulus control tends to improve sleep onset by strengthening bed-sleep association over days.
Indirect effect via improved sleep quality; likely small-to-moderate.

feasibility.notes: Behavioral adherence items may show ceiling/floor effects in some individuals.

predictors.label: Use of scheduled worry time (1–9)
predictors.query_text_used: Use of scheduled worry time (1–9) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: A scheduled daytime worry period ('worry time') can contain and relocate future-oriented worry away from bedtime. | Intervention examples: 10–20 minute scheduled worry slot earlier in the day to capture and plan responses to worries. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Did you use a scheduled 'worry time' today? (1 = not at all, 9 = fully used as planned) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / perseverative_cognition_interventions / worry_management / contain_and_reorient / *constructive_worry_before_bed_worksheet

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Use of scheduled worry time' corresponds to a structured worry-management intervention intended to postpone or contain worry before sleep. Among candidates, the leaf labeled constructive_worry_before_bed_worksheet (PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / sleep_related_cognitive_behavioral_interventions / presleep_mental_skills / detachment_and_unloading / *constructive_worry_before_bed_worksheet) directly maps to a presleep worksheet designed to manage future-oriented worry. The ontology_path explicitly ends with before_bed_worksheet, aligning with the scheduled worry concept, and the measurement item (Did you use a scheduled 'worry time' today?; 1–9 Likert) aligns with applying this construct in an EMA evening prompt. This makes it the most specific, operational leaf for this variable among the candidates.

source_variable.label: Use of scheduled worry time (1–9)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: A scheduled daytime worry period ('worry time') can contain and relocate future-oriented worry away from bedtime.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: 10–20 minute scheduled worry slot earlier in the day to capture and plan responses to worries.

measurement.item_or_signal: Did you use a scheduled 'worry time' today? (1 = not at all, 9 = fully used as planned)
measurement.notes: Designed to capture containment strategy use earlier in the day that may protect evening.

expected_effects.rationale: Containing worries earlier reduces the likelihood of nightly replay; effect moderate.
Directly targets future-oriented worry with large expected effect on same evening.
Reducing worry content reduces cognitive activation at night.
May modestly facilitate sleep onset the same night by lowering pre-sleep worry.
Indirectly reduces morning irritability via improved sleep quality.

feasibility.notes: May be subject to demand characteristics; short item reduces burden.

predictors.label: Sleep environment quality (darkness, temperature, noise) 1–9
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep environment quality (darkness, temperature, noise) 1–9 | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Physical sleep environment influences sleep initiation and maintenance and thereby nighttime arousal and morning mood. | Intervention examples: Blackout curtains, earplugs, cooler bedroom, reduce ambient light/noise. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | How optimal was your sleep environment last night (darkness, temperature, noise)? | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_environment / *sleep_environment

mapping.rationale: Best-match leaf is within BIO sleep_environment domain, specifically light_and_display_control with *low_lux_bedroom_lighting. The variable Sleep environment quality (darkness, temperature, noise) 1–9 is a composite of darkness, etc. Lighting darkness is the most directly captured predictor among candidates; although it doesn't cover temperature/noise, it represents the most specific, actionable operational construct aligned to the darkness aspect of the sleep environment and aligns with the morning-diary self-report measurement of last night’s environment. The measurement mode (EMA_self_report in morning diary) fits the intention of a self-reported environment quality item; thus this is the best leaf under the constraints.

source_variable.label: Sleep environment quality (darkness, temperature, noise) 1–9
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Physical sleep environment influences sleep initiation and maintenance and thereby nighttime arousal and morning mood.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Blackout curtains, earplugs, cooler bedroom, reduce ambient light/noise.

measurement.item_or_signal: How optimal was your sleep environment last night (darkness, temperature, noise)?
measurement.notes: Single composite item to limit burden and capture environment-related variance.

expected_effects.rationale: A better environment reduces disturbances that can trigger replay; effect modest.
A better environment reduces opportunities for worry-associated awakenings.
Improved environment reduces physiological/sensory arousal during night.
Better environment supports earlier sleep onset and sleep continuity.
Better environment tends to improve sleep and reduce next-morning irritability.

feasibility.notes: Single composite is practical and reduces participant burden.

predictors.label: Morning stabilization routine (light + gentle activity) 1–9
predictors.query_text_used: Morning stabilization routine (light + gentle activity) 1–9 | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: A consistent morning anchor (light + gentle mobility) stabilizes circadian rhythms and improves morning mood/regulation. | Intervention examples: Within 60 min of waking: 10–20 min outside or bright light + gentle stretches/walk. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Within the first hour after waking, did you do a morning stabilization routine (light + gentle activity)? | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_routines_and_practices / morning_stabilization / *morning_stabilization

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to an operational morning stabilization routine; among leaves, 'consistent_morning_anchor_routine' directly captures a stable morning anchor tied to circadian entrainment via light cues. The measurement item asks about performing morning stabilization within an hour of waking, matching this leaf's scope. The mechanism aligns with stabilizing circadian timing and morning mood regulation through a consistent light-based anchor.

source_variable.label: Morning stabilization routine (light + gentle activity) 1–9
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: A consistent morning anchor (light + gentle mobility) stabilizes circadian rhythms and improves morning mood/regulation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Within 60 min of waking: 10–20 min outside or bright light + gentle stretches/walk.

measurement.item_or_signal: Within the first hour after waking, did you do a morning stabilization routine (light + gentle activity)?
measurement.notes: Expected to have relatively high immediate influence on morning irritability.

expected_effects.rationale: By stabilizing circadian timing, may indirectly reduce night replay over days.
Indirect effect via improved daytime regulation and sleep timing.
Circadian entrainment supports lower evening hyperarousal over time.
Morning anchor supports phase advance over days to weeks, improving sleep onset timing over time.
Strong immediate effect expected on morning irritability (within hours).

feasibility.notes: Morning items tend to have some missingness but are key to morning affect modeling.
